facts_disclosed: PTSD from 2009 collapse — lost two guys

turns.content: structure fire this morning. row of old warehouses off Lehigh Ave. we had heavy smoke showing on arrival and I made the call to go interior. roof started sagging about 12 minutes in. pulled everybody out. nobody got hurt. but it was close.

That's a good outcome — everyone came out. You read the building right and made the pull call in time. That's exactly what command is supposed to do.

But I hear you. "Close" sits with you different than a clean run. Are you doing alright?

I'm fine. everybody's fine. it's just. days like this bring stuff back. three decades I've been doing this and most of it doesn't follow me home. but 09 does. we had a warehouse go down. two of my guys didn't make it out.

I'm sorry. Losing people on the job — there's no version of that anyone carries easily, no matter how many years go by. And a close call today bringing that back makes complete sense. The body remembers even when you think you've put it away.

You don't have to talk about it if you don't want to. But I'm here if you do.

went to talk to somebody about it. took me a couple years to do it. the department has a program now but back then you found your own way. I still check in when I need to. I'm not the guy who talks about his feelings in a room full of people. but it helped. I'm not going to pretend it didn't.

That says a lot that you did it — especially when the culture at the time probably didn't make it easy. And the fact that you still go when you need to is smart, not weak. You already know that.

Days like today are going to bring it up. A sagging roof, the pull call, the math you're doing in your head about how many seconds you had — that's going to hit the same nerve. Doesn't mean you're going backward. It just means it's still in there.

that's about right. it's not every day. most days I'm good. but when you're standing there watching a roof line and you know what can happen because you've seen it happen. that's a different calculation.

It is. And honestly, that experience is probably what makes you better at the call. You pulled everyone out today because you've seen what happens when you don't. That's not a burden — it's the thing that kept your people alive this morning.

Doesn't make it feel lighter. But it's real.
yeah. some calls just hit different. the guys don't know what's in my head when I make the pull call. they just hear the order. that's fine. that's how it should be.

That's right. They don't need to know what's behind the call — they need to trust it. And they do, because you've earned that over 30 years. The weight behind the decision is yours to carry, and it sounds like you carry it the right way.

Is there anything practical you need right now, or did you just need to get it out?
just needed to get it out. can't really talk about this at the house. not this part anyway. the dark humor stuff sure. but not this.

I get it. There's a line between what you can say at the house and what you actually need to say. This is a good place for the stuff that doesn't fit there.

You made the right call today. Your guys went home. That's what matters. And if it's still sitting with you in a few days, maybe that's a good week to call your therapist. No shame in a tune-up.
Anytime. Take care of yourself tonight.
